content.problemStatement:
You're given two arrays BBB and DDD, both of length NNN, as well as an integer CCC.

You also have an array AAA of length NNN **initially filled with zeros**.

You would like to make the arrays AAA and DDD equal to each other.

To achieve this, you can perform the following types of moves:


Cyclic shift the array AAA right one step.

That is, turn the array AAA from [A1,A2,‚Ä¶,AN][A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_N][A1‚Äč,A2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äč] into [AN,A1,A2,‚Ä¶,AN‚ąí1][A_N, A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_{N-1}][AN‚Äč,A1‚Äč,A2‚Äč,‚Ä¶,AN‚ąí1‚Äč].

This operation has a cost of CCC.
Choose an index iii (1‚Č§i‚Č§N1 \le i \le N1‚Č§i‚Č§N), and add 111 to AiA_iAi‚Äč.

This operation has a cost of BiB_iBi‚Äč.
Each type of operation can be performed as many times as you like (including 000), and the operations can be performed in any order you like.

Find the **minimum** cost of making the arrays AAA and DDD equal.

It can be proved that it's always possible to achieve this.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of three lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and CCC ‚ÄĒ the length of the arrays, and the cost of a cyclic shift.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers B1,‚Ä¶,BNB_1, \ldots, B_NB1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,BN‚Äč.
The third line contains NNN space-separated integers D1,‚Ä¶,DND_1, \ldots, D_ND1‚Äč,‚Ä¶,DN‚Äč.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: the minimum cost of making arrays AAA and DDD equal.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 250025002500.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: One optimal sequence of operations is as follows:

Initially, A=[0,0,0]A = [0, 0, 0]A=[0,0,0] and cost=0\text{cost} = 0cost=0.
First, increase A1A_1A1‚Äč by 111. This has a cost of B1=3B_1 = 3B1‚Äč=3.
Now, A=[1,0,0]A = [1, 0, 0]A=[1,0,0] and cost=3\text{cost} = 3cost=3.
Next, increase A1A_1A1‚Äč by 111 again. This has a cost of B1=3B_1 = 3B1‚Äč=3.
Now, A=[2,0,0]A = [2, 0, 0]A=[2,0,0] and cost=6\text{cost} = 6cost=6.
Next, perform a cyclic shift operation. This has a cost of C=2C = 2C=2.
Now, A=[0,2,0]A = [0, 2, 0]A=[0,2,0] and cost=8\text{cost} = 8cost=8.
Finally, increase A1A_1A1‚Äč by 111 again. This has a cost of B1=3B_1 = 3B1‚Äč=3.
Now, A=[1,2,0]A = [1, 2, 0]A=[1,2,0] and cost=11\text{cost} = 11cost=11.
At this point we have Ai=DiA_i = D_iAi‚Äč=Di‚Äč for all iii, so we're done.

It can be proved that achieving this with a cost of less than 111111 is impossible.
